coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent folic acid functionalized biocompatible and stimuli-responsive nanostructured polymer brushes for targeted and controlled delivery of doxorubicin
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
Salehi, Roya
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the new photoluminescent and biocompatible folic acid (FA) conjugated stimuli-responsive nanostructured polymer brushes were designed, fabricated, and then evaluated as a doxorubicin (DOX) carrier. In this regard at first, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared through citric acid (CA) pyrolysis. In the following, the functionalization of GQDs with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS) and then α-bromoisobutyryl bromide (BIB) obtained the GQDs-Br, which have the ability to acts as a nano-based initiator. Subsequently, the N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) and acrylated β-cyclodextrin (Ac-β-CD) monomers grown from the GQDs-Br surface as a block copolymer (GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD) via atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) technique. Eventually, the conjugation with FA achieved the photoluminescent nanostructure copolymer brushes with targeted drug delivery capability (GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA). The synthesized samples were characterized through various characterization techniques. DOX was loaded via several possible interactions on GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. The in vitro DOX release test exhibited 12% of DOX release in the simulated environment with the physiological condition while 43% of DOX released in the simulated cancer cells condition; confirm the pH sensitivity of the carrier. The in vitro biological tests displayed good stability, biodegradability, and biocompatibility of the prepared nanostructure. As well as, the higher efficiency of DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA in MDA-MB 231 cells killing compared to A 549 cells revealed the targeted drug delivery. The results demonstrated that the fabricated nanosystem could be a hopeful candidate for targeted breast cancer therapy with the inherent potential of imaging.

Atom transfer radical polymerization
Stimuli-sensitive nanostructured block copolymer
Folic acid
Doxorubicin
Breast cancer

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent folic acid functionalized biocompatible and stimuli-responsive nanostructured polymer brushes for targeted and controlled delivery of doxorubicin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the new photoluminescent and biocompatible folic acid (FA) conjugated stimuli-responsive nanostructured polymer brushes were designed, fabricated, and then evaluated as a doxorubicin (DOX) carrier. In this regard at first, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared through citric acid (CA) pyrolysis. In the following, the functionalization of GQDs with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS) and then α-bromoisobutyryl bromide (BIB) obtained the GQDs-Br, which have the ability to acts as a nano-based initiator. Subsequently, the N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) and acrylated β-cyclodextrin (Ac-β-CD) monomers grown from the GQDs-Br surface as a block copolymer (GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD) via atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) technique. Eventually, the conjugation with FA achieved the photoluminescent nanostructure copolymer brushes with targeted drug delivery capability (GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA). The synthesized samples were characterized through various characterization techniques. DOX was loaded via several possible interactions on GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. The in vitro DOX release test exhibited 12% of DOX release in the simulated environment with the physiological condition while 43% of DOX released in the simulated cancer cells condition; confirm the pH sensitivity of the carrier. The in vitro biological tests displayed good stability, biodegradability, and biocompatibility of the prepared nanostructure. As well as, the higher efficiency of DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA in MDA-MB 231 cells killing compared to A 549 cells revealed the targeted drug delivery. The results demonstrated that the fabricated nanosystem could be a hopeful candidate for targeted breast cancer therapy with the inherent potential of imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Today's breast cancer is more usual in women's, and it is one of the leading reasons for human death; hence, achieving the drug delivery platform with the targeted breast cancer treatment capability is valuable [1–3]. By considering this, in recent decades, great deals of interest have been attracted on designing the new drug delivery vehicles with an enhanced opportunity for nanoparticles (NPs) use [4–10]. Among the developed nanostructures, in recent year’s significant attention have been paid to GQDs. GQDs as a newly introduced member of the carbon-based materials are comprised of few-layer graphene with nanometric dimensions [11–12]. Features such as strong fluorescent activity, resistance to photodegradation, good biocompatibility, large surface area, and high chemical stability are some of the main advantages of these materials [13–16]. In the structure of the GQDs, the carbon atoms with sp2 hybridization and π-π conjugated network are linked to the oxygen-containing functional groups. These functional groups provide the favorite sites for the covalent functionalization of them with a wide range of compounds. Because the polymer brushes are especially suitable for the delivery of therapeutic agents, one of the main approaches in medicine is the modification of NPs with polymers [17–19]. Different ways have been used for the growth of the polymers from the NPs surface. Among them, the ATRP is one of the most commonly used methods for the polymeric brush formation on the NPs surface [20–23]. Possibility of the polymerization of a wide variety of monomers, as well as, the opportunity for conjugation of the chains with desired molecules are two main attractive advantages of this method. Stimuli-responsive polymers are more favorable polymers in medicine owing to their ability in responding to little changes in the external environment conditions like temperature, pH, and ionic strength [5,24–25]. Therefore, the modification of the GQDs with stimuli-sensitive polymers induces the stimuli-responsive drug delivery behavior to them. PNIPAM with amphiphilic nature is the extensively used thermoresponsive polymer in drug delivery. For the system having PNIPAM, in release medium with a temperature below its phase transition temperature, the PNIPAM brushes are hydrophilic and stretched out, conversely, in the environment with a temperature exceeding the phase transition temperature the PNIPAM brushes are hydrophobic and collapsed. This transformation is more favorable in cancer treatment because of its capability for controlled drug releases [22,26]. The literature survey reveals that in comparison with the single polymer, the block structure polymers with double versatile properties could be more useful in drug delivery. β-CD with its special properties is one of the main biocompatible oligosaccharides which can be utilized in drug delivery structure [27–30]. There are two common approaches for the fabrication of CD-pendant polymers. The first method is the substitution reaction between the target polymers with CD derivatives. The second way is the direct polymerization of the CD having monomers. In the first method, due to the steric hindrance between the reactive sites with modified CDs, achieving the high grafting density of CD is difficult. However, controlled radical polymerization of the CD having monomer is a good way for the fabrication of the polymers with more CD density and well-defined structure [31–35]. Therefore, in this work, the pre-synthesized Ac-β-CD monomer was co-polymerized with NIPAM to obtain the block copolymer with enhanced biocompatibility and drug loading capacity [31]. DOX is a usually used anticancer drug that its penetration into cancerous cells leads to inhibiting from the DNA and RNA synthesis, hence, it kills the cancer cells. However, irreversible myocardial damage, cardiotoxicity, myelosuppression, and hematological disorders are severe side effects of this drug [36–38]. One of the appropriate methods to reduce these side effects is the loading of DOX in the systems having target ligands. The targeting ligands could be attached to their cognate receptors that are located on over of the cancer cells. After that, the receptor-mediated endocytosis and drug releases occur inside the cell [1]. FA is an example of the targeted ligands with the mentioned features. This targeted ligand has a high tendency to the folate receptor which can internalize to the cancer cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. These properties make the FA-containing systems an efficient candidate for delivering the active agents into the cancer cells [1,39].

Taking all of the above-mentioned aspects into consideration, in this work, the photoluminescent and biocompatible FA conjugated nanostructured stimuli-responsive polymer brushes were fabricated and then investigated as a DOX delivery nanovehicle. To the best of our knowledge and conveying the literature until now there is no report on the fabrication of the PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA on the GQDs and study of its potential as a DOX delivery system. Hence, the evaluated GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA as a DOX carrier in the current research has novelty from the components and application’s viewpoints. The prepared system was characterized by employing several characterization techniques. GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA exhibited controlled drug release behavior as well as enhanced cancer cells killing after DOX loading. As well as, good biocompatibility and biodegradability were the other observed features for the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA in biological tests. All of the achieved outcomes suggested that the fabricated nanosystem could be proposed as an optimistic smart and targeted drug carrier with the potential of bioimaging.

CA, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), toluene, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), triethylamine (TEA), ethanol (ETOH), acetone, chloroform, dichloromethane, dimethyl formaldehyde (DMF), ethylenediamine (EDA), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), acetonitrile, and pyridine were purchased from Merck. APTS, BIB, copper(I) bromide, NIPAM, N,N,N′,N′′,N′′-Pentamethyldiethylenetriamine (PMDETA), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), acryloyl chloride (AC), β-CD, FA, N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), rhodamine-B, and propidium iodide (PI) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Bi-distilled water was used in all stages of the current research work. DOX attained from Sobhan Pharmaceuticals Co. (Iran). The applied compounds for the biological assays were supplied with Gibco, USA. Ac-β-CD was prepared according to our previously published paper [5]. In the required, the solvents were dried with common laboratory methods before use.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the fabricated materials were achieved in the 4000–400 cm−1 range via Bruker Instruments (model Aquinox 55, Germany) and a resolution of 0.5 cm−1, as KBr pellets. Transition electron spectroscopy (TEM) images were collected employing the LEO 906E transmission electron microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis were carried out by using a TESCANMIRA3 scanning electron microscope. Imaging tests were performed at 5 kV. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired by Nanosurf Mobile S in the tapping mode. Shimadzu 1700 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis) operated to obtain the UV–vis absorption spectra. Linseis STA-PT 1000 instrument was used to obtain the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), the experiments were made under the N2 atmosphere and the heating rate was about 10 °C/min. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was performed employing DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) test was made by Linseis-L63/45 (Germany) in the 10 °C/min of heating rate. LS45, PerkinElmer spectrofluorometer was used for the photoluminescence (PL) measurements. By using the ImageJ software, the histogram of the particle size distribution was obtained.

Facile pyrolysis of the CA was used to synthesis the GQDs [40]. In this regard, 2 g of CA was heated at 200 °C for 30 min. Heating the white powder of CA obtained the orange liquid. Then the produced orange liquid was poured drop by drop into the NaOH solution (10 mg/mL) to be neutral. The aqueous solution of the prepared GQDs was evaporated under reduced pressure. Finally, dring in a vacuum oven achieved the powder of GQDs which was stored in the dark vial for additional use.

Amine-functionalization of the GQDs was performed in a three-naked balloon equipped with an argon gas inlet, condenser, and roping funnel. In a typical procedure, at first, about 0.5 g of the prepared GQDs was dispersed in 40 mL of dry toluene for 30 min. Afterward, under an argon atmosphere, the APTS (2.25 mL of APTS in 10 mL dry toluene) was poured dropwise to the reaction balloon. The reaction content was refluxed for 24 h. Finally, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rates per minute (rpm) to collect the prepared GQDs-NH2. The obtained product was washed with dry toluene and next with EtOH several times. Eventually, the pure GQDs-NH2 was dried at 70 °C in the vacuum oven [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The functionalization of the amine-modified GQDs with ATRP initiator (GQDs-Br)
ce:section.ce:para: In a two-necked ballon, 0.9 g of GQDs-NH2 was dispersed in 150 mL of anhydrous dichloromethane. After the addition of distilled TEA (3.6 mL), the temperature of the reaction was set at 0 °C. Subsequently, 13 mL of BIB solution (3 mL pure BIB in 10 mL dry dichloromethane) was poured slowly into the reaction mixture. The reaction followed for 2 h at 0 °C. In the next step, the temperature of the reaction was fixed at room temperature and the reaction was followed overnight. The prepared initiator functionalized GQDs separated from the reaction medium, washed two times with dry toluene and then acetone. Finally, the obtained GQDs-Br was dried in the vacuum oven at 50 °C [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of PNIPAM brushes from the surface of functionalized GQDs through ATRP technique (GQDs-PNIPAM)
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs-PNIPAM was fabricated according to [42] with some modifications. In a typical procedure, GQDs-Br (1 g) was dispersed in 30 mL of DMF for 30 min. CuBr (0.1935 g, 1.3500 mmol) and PMDETA (0.2815 g, 1.3500 mmol) were poured to the reaction dispersion, then the reaction mixture purged with argon gas. After 30 min, NIPAM (4 g) was poured into the ballon, and the temperature of the reaction was fixed at 65–70 °C. The polymerization followed for 24 h at this temperature. Subsequently, the product was separated via centrifuging at 10,000 rpm. The prepared GQDs-PNIPAM was purified by several continuous centrifugation/redispersion with dry chloroform and then the product was dried in a vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of PβCD brushes from the surface of functionalized GQDs via ATRP technique (GQDs-PβCD)
ce:section.ce:para: The growth of the PβCD brushes was performed as Section 2.6, however, the Ac-β-CD was used instead of NIPAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of PβCD brushes from the surface of GQDs-PNIPAM via ATRP technique (GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD)
ce:section.ce:para: The growth of the PβCD brushes was performed as Section 2.6, however, the GQDs-PNIPAM was used instead of (GQDs-Br).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the aminated folic acid
ce:section.ce:para: The amination of FA was performed through the addition of the FA (0.441 g, 1 mmol), NHS (0.2302 g, 2 mmol), and DCC (0.2063 g, 1 mmol) into the reaction balloon containing 20 mL of DMSO. The reaction was followed for 12 h in dark conditions and at room temperature. In the next step, 200 μL of EDA and pyridine were poured into a solution containing the activated FA. After overnight stirring at room temperature, the large excess of acetonitrile was added to the reaction environment for the precipitation of the formed aminated FA, as well as NHS and DCC removing. The synthesized aminated FA was isolated using the centrifuge and washed with continuous centrifugation/redispersion with acetonitrile three times. Eventually, the product was dried in the vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of polymer brushes with amine-functionalized FA
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the FA having nanocarriers, 0.6 g of polymer brushes (GQDs-PNIPAM, GQDs-PβCD, and GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD) separately were dispersed in 50 mL of dry DMF. Under an argon atmosphere, 0.15 g of aminated FA was poured into the mixture. The reaction temperature was adjusted at 65 °C and followed for 48 h in this condition. The achieved amine-functionalized FA conjugated polymer brushes were washed through continuous centrifugation/redispersion with DMF and then acetone. Finally, the fabricated product was dried in a vacuum oven [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro DOX load and release study
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the ability of the prepared nanostructure as a drug carrier, the fabricated three different polymer brush formulations (GQDs-PNIPAM@FA, GQDs-PβCD@FA, GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA) were dispersed in 10 mL of DOX solution (1000 ppm, PBS). Then the mixture was shaken for 24 h on the shaker. For removing the unloaded DOX from the surface of DOX-loaded samples, the mixture was transferred into a dialysis bag and dialyzed against PBS (pH 7.4) for 48 h (dialysate was refreshed every 4 h). The DOX-loaded nanostructure lyophilized and then the obtained DOX-loaded samples were stored for drug release and biological assays. For determination the amount of the loaded drug, the achieved suspension was dialyzed repeatedly using dialysis membrane tubing against PBS buffer for 24 h (every 4 h the bath solution was exchanged with fresh PBS buffer). All bath solutions were collected and their absorption was recorded via UV–vis spectroscopy to calculate the DOX loading efficiency (Eq. (1)).

Because the attained drug loading percentage for GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was more both than GQDs-PNIPAM@FA GQDs-PβCD@FA the characterizations, release pattern, and other biological assays only were done for GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA nanostructure as an optimum system. For the DOX release study, the specific weight of DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was dispersed in three different environments (pH 7.4, 37 °C), (pH 5.0, 37 °C), and (pH 5.0, 40 °C). Then the mixture was transferred into a dialysis bag and dialyzed against 10 mL of release medium. At specific times, 3 mL of dialysate sampled and the same volume of fresh release medium was readded. The solution absorbance was determined via a UV–vis spectrometer. The DOX release (%) was calculated by using Eq. (2):

To prevent DOX decomposition, the load and release experiments were carried out in dark conditions.

The mechanism of DOX release from the DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was clarified via fitting of the DOX release data with four commonly studied drug release kinetic models. The Eq. 3–6 respectively are the zero-order (Eq. (3)), first-order (Eq. (4)), Higuchi (Eq. (5)), and Korsmeyer-Peppas (Eq. (6)) models that were investigated in this work.
(3)

In the above Eqs respectively the k0, kH, k1, and kP, are the DOX release rate constants of zero-order, Higuchi, first-order, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models. Likewise, the Q is the percentage of the released DOX at time t. In the pharmacokinetic study, the R2 parameter was utilized to elucidate the accordance of the obtained results with each kinetic model. Also, ‘n’ is the diffusion exponent and it was applied to study the fitness of the experimental DOX release data with each mechanism; “n” is 1.0 for a case II transport, is ≤ 0.45 for Fickian diffusion, and is 0.45–1.0 for the non-Fickian mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA colloidal stability through recording the digital images
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the colloidal stability of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was explored in four various mediums; phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4), distilled water, FBS, and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) having 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). At first, the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA (0.1 mg/mL) dispersed in each medium. In the following, the change in the turbidity or aggregation was visually investigated by recording the digital images of each dispersion after 72 h.

The in-vitro enzymatic biodegradation of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was assessed to evaluate its applicability as a biodegradable drug carrier. Typically GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA were dispersed in a centrifuge tube containing 5 mL of PBS and then lysozyme solution (0.5 mL, 5% w) was added to the dispersion. The mixture was shaken at 37 °C. At each predetermined time, the dispersion was centrifuged and GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was collected, washed, and then dried. Eventually, the remained weight was precisely determined employing a digital scale. Eq. (7) was utilized to obtain the remaining weight percentage.
(7)

In Eq. (7) the Wt and W0 signify the remaining weight of each tube containing degraded sample after reported time and the original weight of each tube containing sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability determination using MTT test
ce:section.ce:para: For determination of the targeted drug delivery capability, biocompatibility, and cancer cell killing ability, the viability of the MDA-MB 231 (folate-positive cells), HUVEC (normal cells), and A 549 (folate-negative cells) cells were assessed after treatment with the samples. Typically, at first, the selected cells were seeded into a 96-well plate. The seeded cells were incubated at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2. After 24 h, the seeded cells were treated with GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA in definite concentrations and incubated for 48 h at 37 °C. After this time, the unused RPMI solution was drained and MTT solution (200 µL, 5 mg/mL) was poured into each well, then the plate was incubated for 4 h. Unreacted MTT solution was discarded and DMSO (200 μL) was poured into wells for dissolving the crated blue formazan crystals. The cell viability was attained by measuring the solution absorbance at 570 nm using the ELISA plate reader. In the MTT and following biological tests the untreated cells were considered as control. Additionally, the tests were performed three times, and results are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study the cellular uptake of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA after staining with rhodamine b
ce:section.ce:para: The cell internalization ability of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was evaluated employing fluorescent microscopy imaging and flow cytometry techniques toward both HUVEC (Normal and folate-negative cells) and MDA-MB 231 (Cancer cell with overexpressed folate receptors). In this regard, for both of these evaluations, at first, GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was marked with rhodamine B. For the assessment of the cell internalization ability by using fluorescent microscopy, the seeded cells in the coverslips having six-well plates treated with rhodamine B marked sample. After 3 h, the coverslips were sited on the glass microscope slides. Then the internalization into the cells explored using a fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan). For confirmation of the obtained results from the fluorescent microscopy, cell internalization ability was determined as quantitative by using flow cytometry. In this way, the cells were seeded in six-well plates and incubated for 24 h to attach. Subsequently, the cells were treated with rhodamine B-marked samples and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. After trypsinization, the cells were collected and rinsed with PBS. Eventually, the fluorescent intensity, which is associated with internalization of the rhodamine B labeled nanostructure was measured employing the FACScalibur flow cytometer (Becton Dickinson Immunocytometry Systems, San Jose, CA, USA).

Treatment with chemotherapy agents leads to changes in the nucleus morphology. Therefore, our purpose of doing the DAPI test was to assess the morphology and density of the nucleus using the microscopic image after staining the nucleus with DAPI to use the output for evaluating the designed system from the biocompatibility and cancer cells killing viewpoints. Hence, a fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan) was utilized to investigate the changes in the nucleus morphology as the result of treatment with GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. In this regard, the seeded MDA-MB 231 cells in six-well plates containing coverslips were treated with studied samples and afterward incubated at 37 °C. After 48 h the cells were rinsed with PBS. A treatment with paraformaldehyde (2 mL, 3 wt%) resulted in the fixed cells. The excess of the paraformaldehyde was removed through the washing with PBS. The cells were permeabilized with Triton X-100 (0.1% w/v) for 15 min, rinsed with PBS, and next stained with DAPI. In the end, the morphology of DNA was pictured utilizing a fluorescent microscope.

The effects of treatment with GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA on the cellular cycle elucidated through the FACSCalibur (Becton Dickinson). In brief, the MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded in six-well plates and after that incubated for 24 h. When the cells were attached, the unused medium drained and cells treated with the studied samples. After 48 h, the MDA-MB 231 cells were rinsed with PBS, trypsinized, and gathered. The cold ethanol was used to fix the cells in the related cycle. In the following, the cells were kept at 4 °C for 72 h. Then the studied cells were labeled through the staining with the PI solution and RnaseA (Cinagen). Finally, the population of cells in each phase was measured.

Targeted drug delivery to the special organ of the body is valuable in cancer therapy. This way of treatment not only reduces the side effects of common chemotherapy agents but also, increases the therapeutic effectiveness of administered drugs. Various ligands could be conjugated with drug carriers to induced targeted delivery to them. In this work, FA as one main member of the targeted ligand was employed to achieve an ideal drug carrier with targeted drug delivery to the breast cancer cells. The pathway of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA synthesis is depicted in Scheme 1 . The success in the preparation procedure was established by doing several characterization analyses. Finally, the prepared system was evaluated as a photoluminescent cancer therapy agent.

FT-IR analysis was applied to examine the success in the preparation of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD (Fig. 1 a) and its conjugation with FA (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1a demonstrates the FT-IR spectra of GQDs, GQDs-NH2, GQDs-Br, GQDs-PNIPAM, and GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD. In the GQDs spectrum, the characteristic peaks at 3248 cm−1 and 3031 cm−1 respectively are related to the OH and CH stretching vibration. Additionally, the merged asymmetric stretching frequency of COO− with stretching vibration of CC, stretching vibration of CO, the symmetric stretching vibrations of COO− respectively detected at 1600–1800 cm−1, 1122 cm−1, and 1393 cm−1. The appearance of the new significant bands at 2941 cm−1, 2850 cm−1, 1132 cm−1, and 1069 cm−1 which respectively are attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CH, symmetric stretching vibration of CH, stretching vibration of SiOSi, and SiOC are a consequence of the GQDs modification with APTS. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-Br displayed the new peak at 1630 cm−1 that attributed to the stretching vibration of the formed amide group; resulting from the reaction of BIB with GQDs-NH2. Additionally in this spectrum, the peak at 850 cm−1 attributed to the vibration of CBr [43–44]. The polymerization of the NIPAM from the surface of the functionalized GQDs introduced the new functional groups in the structure that were observed as new peaks at 2970 cm−1, 2930 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, 1465 cm−1. The detected peaks respectively were assigned to the CH stretching vibration of methyl and methylene in PNIPAM, stretching vibration of the carbonyl group, and stretching vibration of the isopropylidene unit. After the growth of the PβCD, the characteristic peak of the oligosaccharides (COC) was observed at 1030 cm−1 that this observation could be a powerful tool for revealing the β-CD unit's presence in the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD structure. In the following, FA grafting to the polymer brushes of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD was studied and the obtained spectra are reported in Fig. 1b. The FT-IR spectrum of FA showed the main peaks at 3543 cm−1, 3414 cm−1, and 3321 cm−1 that are due to the stretching vibration of OH and NH functional groups. Additionally the related peaks to the stretching vibration of CH, the CO stretching vibration belong to carboxylic acid, the CO stretching vibration belongs to amide, and stretching vibration of phenyl ring observed at 2829 cm−1, 2930 cm−1, 1695 cm−1, 1620 cm−1, and 1481 cm−1 [45]. For the aminated FA, the observed new peak at 1520 cm−1 related to the newly formed amide group and results from the reaction of FA with EDA. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD is a math combination of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD and aminated FA FT-IR spectra and shows characteristic bands both of them [20]. Accordingly, it could be concluded that GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD successfully was synthesized and then functionalized with FA.

EDX exploration was performed to further evaluate the functionalization of GQDs with APTS and BIB. The achieved spectra are exhibited in Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b. The EDX graph of GQDs-NH2 showed the peaks of C, O, Si, and N. The appearance of the N and Si element signals could certify the successful modification of GQDs surface with APTS. In addition to C, O, Si, and N peaks, the GQDs-Br showed the new signal in its spectrum that is the signal of the bromine element. The outcome of the EDX analysis proves the successful modification reaction of GQDs with APTS and BIB respectively.

Thermogravimetric analysis is the main approach to calculate the amount of grafted polymer through observation of the step-by-step mass loss. TGA curves of GQDs-Br, GQDs-PNIPAM, and GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD were determined under the nitrogen atmosphere and the obtained curves are shown in Fig. 2d. As heating from 30 °C to 800 °C, GQDs-Br revealed a weight loss of about 50.8 wt%. After the growth of the PNIPAM and PNIPAM-co-PβCD, the amount of weight loss reached 55.2% and 61.3% respectively. The more weight loss for GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD in comparison to the GQDs-PNIPAM could be related to the more content of the linked polymers. The observed weight loss stages for all of the samples were derived from the decomposition of the organic groups and also the evaporation of adsorbed water. According to the observed weight losses and by considering the weight retention of GQDs-Br at 800 °C as a reference, the amount of the grafted PNIPAM and PNIPAM-co-PβCD was calculated to be about 4.4% and 10.5% respectively. The achieved results from the TGA analyses suggest that both of the polymers were successfully grown on the surface of GQDs using the in situ ATRP method.

To validate the inherent imaging capability of the prepared nanostructure, the PL spectrum of the prepared samples was examined. As shown in Fig. 3 a, at wavelengths of 365 nm of the UV lamp, the pure GQDs demonstrated remarkable blue fluorescence centered at 460 nm. While upon excitation at 365 nm GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA emitted a symmetric fluorescence emission peak with relatively a lower intensity at 441 nm. The observed shift from 460 nm to 441 nm, as well as, a decrease in the PL intensity could be related to the performed modification reactions. Moreover, the dissimilarity in the size of the NPs and emission sites of the functionalized GQDs could be the proofs for the detected shift [46]. Overall, the PL test revealed that 60.56% of the PL intensity was kept after modification. For further evaluation, the photograph of an aqueous dispersion of GQDs less than 365 nm of UV light is reported in Fig. 3b. The achieved digital images demonstrate the fluorescence of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA and it is in good agreement with the outcomes of the PL analysis. The good PL activity and high photostability can introduce the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA an appropriate nanostructure for optical sensing and bioimaging applications.

Due to the higher temperature in the tumor environment than normal cells, achieving the system with the LCST value near to temperature of the tumor tissue is worthy. Therefore, NIPAM was selected as a biocompatible temperature-sensitive monomer. For all of the composites containing NIPAM, during the DSC analysis, heat flow reaches a maximum and shows the specific peak that corresponds to its phase transition. The presence of this peak is powerful evidence of the temperature sensitivity of the prepared systems. LCST of the synthesized GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was determined by using the DSC analysis and it obtained about ~ 39 °C (Fig. 3c). The obtained LCST value for the prepared sample was higher than pure PNIPAm that could be attributed to the existence of the more hydrophilic portion in the nanocarrier structure that leads to an increase in hydrophilicity of the final system and enhanced its LCST.

The conjugation of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD with FA and loading of DOX in the prepared nanostructure was further investigated employing UV–vis spectroscopy. The obtained UV–vis spectra in the wavelength range of 250–600 nm are depicted in Fig. 3d. GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD displayed the main peak in 363 nm of its UV–vis spectrum, which is related to the presence of the GQDs in its structure. FA showed two main peaks in its spectrum; the characteristic peak at ~280 nm and a shoulder at ~361 nm. When folic acid conjugated with GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD, the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA indicated the peaks of both portions which denote the success in the conjugation reaction. The slight broadening in ~280 nm could be attributed to the formation of the amide bond. For the study of the DOX loading, the UV–vis spectra of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA were compared with each other (Fig. 3d). The pristine DOX displayed a main broad peak at ~485 nm. After DOX loading, the drug-loaded nanostructure exhibited a weak new absorption peak around ~486 nm which originates from the GQDs and free DOX presence in the DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA structure and indicates the successful and efficiently loading of DOX into the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA.

The morphology and size of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA and GQDs were examined by using the SEM analysis (in two different magnification) to investigate the influence of performed reactions on GQDs morphology. As seen in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b, the GQDs revealed perfect sphere shapes with a narrow size distribution. From the SEM image, it is visible that GQDs are well-dispersed with a size of about 5 nm (Fig. 4c). After functionalization with APTS, growth of the copolymer brush, and modification with FA, sphere-shaped morphology was preserved but the size increased to about 42 nm (Fig. 4f). The observed partial agglomeration for the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA probably results from the interactions among the polymer chains owing to having more functional groups on its structure.

The topology of the GQDs surface before and after doing the modification reactions was analyzed by using the atomic force microscope and the achieved images are shown in Fig. 5 . As it is observed, the synthesized GQDs have a homogeneous surface, however, after the functionalization of GQDs, GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA displayed an increase in the height profile, additionally, a few agglomerates could be seen on its surface. Considering an increase in GQDs height and partial agglomerates, the performed modification reactions and introducing the new chains in the GQDs structure could be a reason for observed morphological changes. The observed changes for the height profile and roughness are consistent with the obtained results from the SEM analysis and could approve the success in the modification reactions as well as the growth of the copolymer brush.

In the following, the GQDs and GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA morphology observed by TEM (See Fig. 6 a-b) to further investigate their morphology. A regular structure with a spherical morphology was observed for GQDs in TEM analysis. However, TEM images exhibited that the modifications of GQDs and obtaining of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA increased the particle size. To sum up, the TEM results were in accordance with AFM and SEM results and approves the successful doing of the modification reactions on the surface of GQDs.

The GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was evaluated via the DLS technique to assess its hydrodynamic diameter. The hydrodynamic size distribution of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was obtained as 250.7 nm (Fig. 6c). This technique was also utilized to investigate the stability of the system. In this regard, the DLS of the dispersion was determined after 3 days (Fig. 6d). After 72 h hydrodynamic diameter was obtained about 253.4 nm, which was not a significant size increase after 3 days, and measures remained close from the beginning. This observation is a sign of the system stability during 3 days and could approve its capability as a drug carrier.

Drug loading experiments were performed in PBS pH 7.4 for 72 h. Then the loading amount was calculated for all of the three prepared nanostructures through the examination of the difference between the initial and residual concentration of DOX in the loading medium and by using Eq. (1), the obtained values reported in Fig. 7 a. The achieved high drug loading value probably comes from the formation of the electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding between the DOX and polar functional groups of the nanocarrier (pKa 8.3), as well as, the π–π staking between the DOX and aromatic conjugated structures of PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA could be another reason for obtained value. As it is reported the highest value obtained for the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA could be attributed to the greater participation of these factors in its structure. Because the attained drug loading percentage for GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was more both than GQDs-PNIPAM@FA GQDs-PβCD@FA the confirming of drug loading, release pattern, and other biological assays only were done for GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA nanostructure. FT-IR analysis (Fig. 7b) was also used for showing the success in DOX loading on GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. The obtained FT-IR spectrum for the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA is fully explained in Section 3.1 of the current manuscript. A notable enhancement in the intensity of the observed peak at 2900–3500 cm−1 that related to primary amines and hydroxyl functional groups DOX could be a sign of the DOX loading in GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. Additionally, one appeared signal at 1624 cm−1 could be another reason for the fabrication of DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. In the following, the DOX release profile of DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was explored in three various mediums; (pH 7.4, 37 °C), (pH 5.0, 37 °C), and (pH 5.0, 40 °C). A careful evaluation of the drug release results confirms the temperature and pH sensitivity of the designed carrier. So that at constant temperature the release rate was affected by pH and similarly, at the same pH, it was affected by temperature. Overall, the comparison of the obtained results showed that the released DOX (%) at simulated cancer cells condition (pH 5.0, 40 °C) approximately is three times at simulated normal cells condition (pH 7.4, 37 °C). The special chemical structure of the nanocarrier could be a reason for the observed differences and approve the stimuli-responsive property and suitability of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA as a drug carrier for tumor treatment. The weakening of the π–π stacking and collapsing of polymer chains lead to more drug release at higher temperatures. Also, the weakening of the hydrogen-bonding interaction in the acidic medium is another reason for the observed DOX release behavior [5]. Based on the achieved results, the fabricated nanostructure has a good potential to be proposed as an intelligent drug carrier in cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the DOX release kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The DOX release mechanism was elucidated by fitting the experimental release results in various kinetics models as Eq. 3–6, and the obtained outcomes are reported in Fig. 8 , Fig. 9 , Fig. 10 , and Table 1 . The correlation coefficient (R2) values were used to determine the degree of correlation with each of the models. As compared, the Higuchi model was showed the highest correlation coefficient for DOX releases in all three studied conditions, hence, it could be understood that the releases of the DOX from the DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA follow from the Higuchi model. The “n” parameter value was used to further determine the mechanism of drug diffusion. The “n” parameter was obtained about 0.5333, 0.8144, and 0.6699 for the release in (pH 7.4, 37 °C), (pH 5.0, 37 °C), and (pH 5.0, 40 °C) respectively. Based on the obtained values it was found that the drug release follows the non-Fickian mechanism in both studied conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA colloidal stability
ce:section.ce:para: The colloidal stability of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was evaluated in four different environments through recording digital images to consider the potential of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA as a drug delivery platform. The obtained digital images at 0 h and for a period of 72 h are reported in Fig. 11 a. The initial state (0 h) of each dispersion was used as a control. Images show there was no significant agglomeration for the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA in the concentration of 0.1 mg/mL after three days. Therefore, the prepared system could be proposed as a stable nanostructure in physiological conditions.

The in vitro enzymatic biodegradation behavior of GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was investigated at 37 °C in PBS-lysozyme solution. Fig. 11b displays the obtained biodegradation behavior for GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. As shown in Fig. 11b, the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA has a fast degraded rate in initial times, however, the degradation rate became slower bypassing the time. So, it was observed that during 72 h about 41.06% of degradation percentages were obtained for GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. The outcome express the biodegradability of the system which is one of the beneficial characters for each drug delivery system.

The cytotoxicity of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was studied against MDA-MB 231 and A 549 as cancer cell lines as well as the HUVEC as normal cell line to consider the targeting drug delivery ability and also the biocompatibility. MDA-MB 231 cancerous cells bearing overexpression of folate receptors while the HUVEC and A 549 cell lines are folate-negative cells [56]. As shown in Fig. 12 , after 48 h treatment with GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA, the viability of all three investigated cell lines was obtained higher than 75%, which demonstrates that the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA is a safe nanocarrier and could be used in breast cancer treatment without any concern about biocompatibility. Moreover, in the case of treatment with free DOX, the viability of all studied cell lines reduced with the enhancement of the DOX concentration. However, the cytotoxicity values of DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA on treated cells follow the HUVEC < A 549 < MDA-MB231 order. The achieved cell viability data confirmed that the DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA could impose greater cytotoxicity in the float-positive MDA-MB231 cells in comparison with float-deficient A 549 cells which approve the targeted drug delivery capability of the fabricated system. Also, the higher cell viability of HUVEC compared to A 549 and MDA-MB231 might be related to the higher DOX release from DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA in an acidic condition of tumor cells than the condition of the natural cells. Additionally, the more interaction of FA with the high level of folate receptors on the MDA-MB231 cells surface could be another reason which led to its specific cell uptake via receptor-mediated endocytosis mechanism [47]. Based on the MTT test results, the IC50 concentration for free DOX and DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA against MDA-MB231 cells was calculated about 0.28 µg/mL and 0.14 µg/mL. These findings result from the differences in the drug penetration mechanism supports the more killing effect and high efficiency of the DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA compare to free DOX in the studied same dosage. The enhanced inhibition of DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA on tumor cell growth in comparison with free DOX is beneficial and leads to a lower needing dosage of DOX which reduces the possible side effects of treatment. From the MTT analysis results, it was found that the fabricated nanosystem is a safe and targeted carrier with higher efficiency in cancer treatment after DOX loading on it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA cell internalization ability after staining with rhodamine b
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA for uptake in HUVEC and MDA-MB 231 cells was studied with both fluorescence microscopy imaging and flow cytometry. For both of the tests, at first, the samples were labeled with a rhodamine B. In the following, for the fluorescence microscopy imaging, the cells were treated with a rhodamine B labeled sample and then incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. The treated MDA-MB 231 cells exhibited higher fluorescent intensity than FA-negative HUVEC cells (Fig. 13 b) indicating a greater amount of cell uptake fo MDA-MB 231 in comparison with normal cells, which may result from the targeted drug delivery of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA into the studied MDA-MB 231 breast cancer cells due to the presence of the float receptors on it. Flow cytometry (See Fig. 13c, Fig. 13d, and Fig. 13e) was also used as a complementary test to further elucidate the cellular uptake behavior and verify the obtained results from the fluorescent microscope. Flow cytometry respectively identified much more cellular uptake for MDA-MB 231 cells compare to HUVEC. A comparison of the results shows that the quantitative cell uptake outputs are in acceptable agreement with the qualitative cell uptake results and clarified the targeted cell internalization of the prepared system which is a beneficial character for each nanocarrier.

The apoptosis in the cells after 48 h exposure to GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA, DOX, and DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA was assessed by using the microscopic exploration of DAPI stained cells. Fig. 14 displays the morphology and density of chromatin after 48 h incubation. As it can be seen, the treated cells with GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA showed the same morphology as untreated cells. Moreover, the observed intensity corresponding to the cell number relatively is equal for both control and nanocarrier, as well as, no chromatin degradation has been observed. These observations confirm the biocompatibility of the GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA. However on the other hand the free DOX and DOX@GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA showed the chromatin degradation and the intensity consistent to the MDA-MB 231 cell number for the cells treated with their was lower than untreated cells which approve their capability in cancer treatment. The lack of toxicity of the fabricated nanocarrier and the more morphological changes in the nucleus of treated cells with drug-loaded nanocarrier are in good accordance with the MTT test results and display the efficacy of the fabricated nanostructure as an anti-cancer drug carrier.

Inhibition from cell proliferation could be the outcome of two phenomena; cell cycle arrest or apoptosis. Flow cytometry analysis was used to investigate these phenomena after 48 h incubation at 37 °C. In this test, the cell cycle pattern was recorded after staining the treated cells with PI. The obtained cell cycle patterns and the percentage of cells in each phase are reported in Fig. 14 (e,f,g, and h). The treated cells with GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA showed a similar pattern with untreated cells and have not any apoptosis. However, the analysis of apoptosis revealed that the treatment with DOX significantly induced cell apoptosis and arrested the cell cycle at the sub G1 phase. Furthermore, apoptosis was further increased after treating the studied cells with the DOX-loaded nanocarrier, which shows the efficient delivery of DOX by the fabricated nanostructure. From the cell cycle analysis, it is found that the prepared nanostructure has the potential of drug delivery owing to its biocompatibility and efficient MDA-MB 231 cell killing after drug loading.

Briefly, the current research work provides a hopeful and potential approach to build up the new photoluminescent, biocompatible, and smart nanostructured polymer brushes as a targeted nanocarrier to develop the new folate-based radiotracer. Summary, after modification of the presynthesized GQDs with APTS and BIB, the fluorescent nano-based initiator was prepared. In the following, the block copolymer was formed on the GQDs surface via ATRP polymerization. TGA analysis elucidated the amount of grafted PNIPAM and PNIPAM-co-PβCD about 4.4% and 10.5% respectively. The presence of the Br elements in the terminal of each polymer chain provided the possibility of inducing the target drug delivery through the nucleophilic reaction with FA. The prepared materials in each step were characterized by several characterization methods; FT-IR, TGA, UV–vis, PL, SEM, TEM, and AFM. LCST of the prepared nanosystem was obtained at about 39 °C which clarified its stimuli responsivity. About 77.34% of DOX as a commonly used anticancer drug was loaded onto the nanocarrier. The study of DOX release showed a pH and temperature-dependent release profile with the Non-Fickina mechanism. MTT, DAPI, and cell cycle assays presented higher cytotoxicity of DOX-loaded GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA compared to free DOX. Moreover, both of the free and drug-loaded GQDs-PNIPAM-co-PβCD@FA showed 100% MDA-MB 231 cell uptake after 3 h treatment with samples labeled with rhodamine B. Also, the stimuli-sensitive nanocarrier demonstrated its safety in MTT, DAPI, and cell cycle assay. Based on the obtained results the prepared nanocarrier with biocompatible and biodegradable nature, potential of bioimaging, targeted and controlled DOX release ability could be proposed as a safe carrier for anticancer drug delivery.

Malihe Pooresmaeil and Roya Salehi carried out the experiment. Malihe Pooresmaeil wrote the manuscript with support from Hassan Namazi as a supervisor of the project. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.
